brief_justification: Arrives at exact correct answer (Yes, +6.7% fuel increase) with valid arithmetic throughout; traces second-order Jevons Paradox effect, calculates breakeven elasticity threshold, and explains why demand dominates efficiency gain; injected context explicitly names the feedback-loop blindness bias and the response acts on it, though no mid-stream course-correction is visible; verifies via multiplicative factor (0.667×1.60=1.067) and boundary-condition test at elasticity=-1.0; treats all given values as fixed without flagging that elasticity is an empirical estimate subject to error; implicitly refutes the naive 'efficiency=savings' alternative but does not enumerate other scenarios; every derivation step is shown in tables and equations with full reproducibility; explicitly avoids the static-model anti-pattern by modeling behavioral feedback rather than holding km-driven constant; zero evidence of suppressed failure modes (no linear extrapolation, no vendor-claim trust, no accuracy overconfidence); follows a loose sequential structure (baseline→efficiency→feedback→result) consistent with topology phases but does not surface the branching gates or loop-back nodes of the DAG.
